does: This paper proposes Self-Examining Reinforcement Learning (SERL), a framework where a single large language model functions simultaneously as both an actor generating responses and a judge evaluating them, eliminating the need for external reward signals. The method introduces two complementary reward mechanisms: Copeland-style pairwise comparisons that assess response quality to train the actor, and a self-consistency reward that encourages the judge to make coherent evaluations across different contexts, thereby improving judge reliability. The approach iteratively refines both components in a self-improving loop, with experiments demonstrating performance gains over existing methods on open-domain tasks where subjective evaluation would normally prevent standard reinforcement learning approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research applying reinforcement learning to large language models, extending beyond prior approaches like Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) which require objective ground truth, and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) which depends on external human evaluators. It responds to the challenge of applying RL to subjective open-domain tasks by combining insights from preference learning and self-training methodologies. The use of Copeland-style pairwise comparisons draws on preference-based learning literature, while the self-consistency mechanism echoes work on improving model reliability through internal consistency checks, adapting these concepts to enable autonomous improvement without external supervision.
enables: This framework opens possibilities for scaling LLM improvement beyond the constraints of human annotation and verifiable ground truth, particularly valuable for creative and open-ended tasks where external rewards are difficult to define. The dual-improvement mechanism—where judge reliability feeds back into actor training—could inspire further research into self-referential learning systems and internal oversight mechanisms in LLMs. Future work might extend SERL to multi-turn interactive tasks, explore how to prevent collapse or degradation in the self-improving loop, and investigate whether similar self-examining frameworks could address other alignment challenges where external evaluation is impractical or expensive.
